name: Cylance
funding: $177M raised before $1.4B acquisition by BlackBerry (2019); resold for $160M in 2024
summary: Pioneer of AI/machine-learning-based antivirus that replaced signature detection with predictive math models. BlackBerry bought Cylance for $1.4B in 2019 hoping to pivot into cybersecurity, but the product failed to keep pace with EDR/XDR competitors and BlackBerry sold the Cylance endpoint business to Arctic Wolf in December 2024 for just $160M cash and stock -- roughly 11% of the original price.
lesson: A breakthrough AI detection model is not a durable moat once every competitor adds machine learning; an acquirer's failure to invest in the roadmap can destroy nearly 90% of a deal's value in five years.
source: TechCrunch, 'BlackBerry sells Cylance for $160M, a fraction of the $1.4B it paid in 2018' (Dec 2024); Arctic Wolf press release

name: Cybereason
funding: over $1B raised, including a $325M round in 2019 and $275M in 2021 from SoftBank
summary: AI-driven EDR/XDR platform (MalOp detection engine) that reached a $3.3B valuation in 2021 on SoftBank backing. After SoftBank's WeWork-driven cutbacks forced Cybereason to prioritize profitability, the company underwent three rounds of layoffs (2022-2024), replaced its founding CEO, and saw its valuation collapse roughly 90% to about $300M by 2023.
lesson: Hyper-growth funded by a single strategic investor with its own liquidity problems (SoftBank/WeWork) can force a security vendor into abrupt, value-destroying downsizing regardless of product quality.
source: BankInfoSecurity, 'Cybereason Doing Its 3rd Round of Layoffs Since 2022'; GovInfoSecurity valuation reporting

name: ZeroFox
funding: raised ~$300M+ pre-IPO; SPAC-listed 2022; taken private for $350M enterprise value in 2024
summary: AI-powered external threat intelligence and social media/digital risk protection platform. Went public via SPAC in 2022, but the stock never recovered from a depressed valuation, and in 2024 private equity firm Haveli Investments took the company private for roughly $350M -- down from a reported ~$1.4B valuation at SPAC announcement.
lesson: SPAC listings let richly-valued AI security startups access public markets before their unit economics were proven, and the market later re-rated them sharply downward, ending in a distressed take-private.
source: SecurityWeek, 'ZeroFox to be Taken Private in $350 Million Deal' (Feb 2024)

name: Tessian
funding: $133M raised across six rounds, including a $65M Series C in 2021 at a $500M valuation
summary: London-based AI email security company using behavioral machine learning to stop phishing and misdirected email. Acquired by Proofpoint in December 2023 in a deal analysts estimated at $307M-$415M -- a down round from its last private valuation of roughly $500M-$550M.
lesson: Crowded email-security markets with entrenched incumbents (Proofpoint, Mimecast, Microsoft) can force even well-funded AI point solutions into acquisitions priced below their last private valuation.
source: SecurityWeek / IT-Harvest, 'Proofpoint to Acquire Tessian for AI-Powered Email Security Tech' (Oct 2023)

name: IronNet
funding: over $400M raised, including SPAC proceeds; public via SPAC in 2021
summary: AI-driven network detection and response (Collective Defense) company founded by former NSA director Gen. Keith Alexander. Despite raising over $400M, IronNet posted losses of $242.6M and $111M in its final two fiscal years, laid off staff repeatedly, and ceased all operations in October 2023, filing for Chapter 7 bankruptcy with only $77,000 in assets against $33.8M in debts. Investors also sued Alexander alleging inflated government-contract projections.
lesson: A prestigious founder pedigree and heavy government-contract narratives cannot substitute for real revenue; overstated pipeline claims plus a premature SPAC listing accelerated a total collapse.
source: TechCrunch, 'IronNet, founded by former NSA director, shuts down and lays off staff' (Oct 2023); Bloomberg Law bankruptcy coverage

name: Cyren
funding: publicly traded (Nasdaq: CYRN); valuation fell 89.7% in 2022 to about $5M before collapse
summary: Threat-intelligence and AI/ML-driven email and web security vendor (formerly Commtouch) providing anti-phishing and anti-malware feeds used by other security products. After failing to secure financing or a buyer, Cyren laid off virtually its entire 121-person workforce in February 2023, filed for bankruptcy/liquidation in Israel, the US, UK, Germany and Iceland, and was delisted from Nasdaq; its IP was sold off piecemeal to Data443 and Opin Kerfi.
lesson: A legacy threat-intelligence vendor that never modernized its go-to-market can watch its equity value evaporate almost entirely (to ~$5M) even after decades in business, ending in fire-sale liquidation of the IP.
source: BankInfoSecurity, 'Cyren Ceases Operations After Financing, Sale Efforts Fail' (2023)

name: Norse Corporation
funding: reported $30M+ raised, including a $12M investment from KPMG Capital months before collapse
summary: Big-data threat-intelligence company that claimed to use a global sensor network and analytics to visualize live cyberattacks. After failing to ship promised product capabilities that sales teams had already sold, missing revenue targets, and burning through fresh KPMG funding, Norse laid off ~30% of staff in January 2016, fired its CEO, and effectively disbanded within weeks.
lesson: Flashy live-attack-map marketing does not equal a sellable product; a sales organization promising features engineering hasn't built is a leading indicator of collapse.
source: Krebs on Security, 'Sources: Security Firm Norse Corp. Imploding' (Jan 2016); Forbes CEO interview

name: Cymmetria
funding: approximately $14M raised before being acquired by Stage Fund in 2019
summary: Israeli deception-technology startup (MazeRunner) whose decoy/honeypot network used behavioral analytics to catch nation-state intrusions. Acquired by private equity firm Stage Fund in September 2019, but within months Stage Fund's chairman ordered the Israeli R&D team dismissed; employees filed for company liquidation in December 2019 over roughly $285,600 in unpaid wages.
lesson: An acquisition is not always a successful exit -- a financial buyer with no operating plan for the acquired team can turn what looked like a soft landing into full liquidation within months.
source: Calcalist (CTech), 'Employees of Cyber Deception Company Cymmetria File for Liquidation' (2019)

name: vArmour
funding: over $300M raised across its lifetime
summary: AI-driven application relationship/microsegmentation and cyber-resilience platform. After burning through more than $300M, cycling through multiple CEOs (Eades departed Dec 2022, then NightDragon-installed CEO Matt Gyde left after only months in 2023), and failing to convert a 2023 Series B raise into durable growth, vArmour's assets and patents were sold to recovery firm Fenix24 in January 2025.
lesson: Repeated executive churn and a strategy pivot funded by ever-larger raises ($300M+ total) without matching revenue traction is a classic pattern that ends in a distressed asset sale rather than an exit.
source: PRNewswire, 'Fenix24 Acquires vArmour to Boost Cyber Resiliency and Network Insights' (Jan 2025)

name: Skybox Security
funding: over $330M raised; reportedly generated $500M+ in cumulative revenue
summary: Israeli attack-simulation and exposure/vulnerability-management analytics pioneer. Despite raising over $330M and building substantial revenue, Skybox abruptly shut down on February 24, 2025, laying off all ~300 employees in Israel and the US with no warning, after selling its technology and business assets to rival Tufin in a selective deal that did not assume Skybox's customer contracts.
lesson: A once-pioneering attack-simulation vendor can stagnate as newer AI-native exposure-management platforms overtake it; even substantial historical revenue does not prevent an overnight, no-warning shutdown when the cash runs out.
source: SecurityWeek, 'Skybox Security Shuts Down, Lays Off Entire Workforce' (Feb 2025); Calcalist, 'The fall of Skybox Security'

name: SparkCognition
funding: over $200M raised across multiple rounds
summary: Austin-based AI platform applying machine learning to industrial and cybersecurity use cases (including AI-driven endpoint/anomaly protection). After over-hiring during the AI boom, SparkCognition conducted mass layoffs and restructured its business, eventually rebranding its core operations as Avathon and narrowing its focus away from broad cybersecurity AI applications.
lesson: Positioning as a broad 'AI for everything' platform -- spanning security, industrial IoT, and finance -- diluted product focus, and the eventual rebrand away from the original brand signaled the original strategy did not scale profitably.
source: Glassdoor employee reviews (Avathon/SparkCognition mass layoff); company rebrand press coverage

name: Cofense
funding: acquired out of PhishMe by a BlackRock-led consortium for ~$400M in 2018; rebranded as Cofense
summary: AI/ML-assisted phishing detection and response platform (crowdsourced threat intel plus automated triage) formerly known as PhishMe. Since its 2018 buyout, Cofense has cut staff across three rounds of layoffs within roughly 15 months, with employees and former staff describing recurring financial losses and management instability, though the company continues to operate.
lesson: A private-equity-style buyout at a high valuation can load a company with expectations it cannot meet organically, leading to a slow-motion decline of repeated layoffs rather than a single dramatic failure.
source: Glassdoor employee reviews; Channel Futures coverage of Cofense post-acquisition

name: Lacework
funding: $1.9B+ raised, including a $1.3B round in Nov 2021 at an $8.3B valuation
summary: Cloud security platform whose Polygraph engine used unsupervised machine learning/behavioral anomaly detection to baseline cloud workloads without manual rules. After raising at an $8.3B valuation in the 2021 boom, Lacework cut 20% of staff in 2022 and headcount fell 35% by late 2023; by April 2024 it was in talks to sell to rival Wiz for just $150M-$200M -- a roughly 98% valuation collapse.
lesson: Raising at a peak-bubble valuation on the promise of ML-driven anomaly detection created expectations the business could never grow into once markets reset, producing one of cybersecurity's most extreme down-round collapses.
source: TechCrunch, 'Lacework, last valued at $8.3B, is in talks to sell for just $150-200M' (Apr 2024)

name: Trustwave
funding: acquired by Singtel for $810M in 2015; sold in 2023/2024 for $205M
summary: Managed detection and response provider using AI/ML-driven threat analytics (SpiderLabs threat intelligence) for enterprise clients. Singtel bought Trustwave for $810M in 2015, later took a S$336M impairment charge on the investment, and ultimately sold the business to Chertoff Group affiliate MC2 Titanium for just $205M -- roughly a quarter of the original purchase price.
lesson: A strategic acquirer overpaying for an AI-enabled security platform outside its core telecom business can be forced into a value-destroying divestiture years later once growth fails to materialize.
source: Total Telecom, 'Singtel to sell cybersecurity firm Trustwave for $205 million' (2023)

name: Jumio
funding: over $50M raised before 2016 bankruptcy; assets acquired by Centana Growth Partners
summary: AI-driven identity verification and document/biometric fraud-detection company. Founder and CEO Daniel Mattes grossly overstated 2013-2014 revenue (claiming $251M against a real $17.2M) while secretly selling ~$14M of his personal shares to investors. After Mattes resigned in 2015 and the fraud was uncovered, Jumio filed for bankruptcy in 2016; Centana Growth Partners acquired the remaining assets, and the SEC later fined Mattes $17M.
lesson: Founder-perpetrated financial fraud in a high-growth fraud-prevention company is a stark irony -- investor due diligence on revenue claims matters as much for AI security vendors as for any other startup.
source: SEC v. Daniel Mattes press release (2019); Fortune, 'Jumio Founder Settles $17 Million Fraud Case' (2019)

name: Exabeam
funding: over $325M raised, including a 2021 round at a ~$2.4B valuation; merged with LogRhythm in 2024
summary: AI/ML-driven UEBA and next-gen SIEM platform that automated behavioral threat detection. Facing waning demand as Microsoft and platform consolidation squeezed point-solution SIEM vendors, Exabeam was forced into a 2024 merger with Thoma Bravo-owned rival LogRhythm in which Exabeam's own shareholders had their shares cancelled for no consideration -- triggering shareholder litigation -- followed by 30% workforce cuts in the combined company.
lesson: Being acquired by your own competitor's private-equity owner in a 'merger of equals' framing can mask what is functionally a distressed wind-down, especially when existing shareholders receive nothing.
source: The Register, 'Merged Exabeam and LogRhythm cut jobs, face lawsuit' (Jul 2024)

name: Code42
funding: over $125M raised across its history
summary: Insider-risk and data-loss-detection platform (Incydr) that uses behavioral analytics to flag risky file movement. After selling its legacy CrashPlan backup business -- more than half its revenue -- while retaining most of the associated costs, Code42 conducted layoffs of roughly 15% of staff in both November 2022 and July 2023 as new sales failed to offset the lost revenue base.
lesson: Divesting a legacy cash-generating business to focus on a newer AI-driven security product line can backfire if the cost structure doesn't shrink in proportion to the lost revenue.
source: Star Tribune, 'Minneapolis tech firm Code42 lays off 15% of workforce'; Blocks & Files coverage (2023)

name: Uptycs
funding: over $400M raised
summary: AI-driven cloud and endpoint security analytics platform (unified CNAPP/XDR). Despite raising substantial venture capital, former employees describe continuous, unannounced rounds of layoffs and leadership churn stretching over multiple years as the company struggled to convert its telemetry-analytics platform into durable enterprise revenue amid an increasingly crowded CNAPP market.
lesson: Chronic, unannounced layoffs rather than one clean restructuring event are often a sign that a well-funded AI security platform has not found a repeatable path to profitability.
source: Glassdoor employee reviews, Uptycs (2024-2025)

name: Netography
funding: over $60M raised
summary: AI/ML-powered network detection and response (NDR) startup providing cloud-scale network traffic analytics without deployed sensors. Netography cut staff, including its marketing department, in February 2024 as part of a broader wave of cybersecurity venture belt-tightening, reflecting the difficulty smaller NDR entrants faced competing against larger, better-capitalized platforms.
lesson: In a capital-intensive category like network detection and response, a smaller AI-native entrant can be squeezed by funding downturns well before its product differentiation has time to compound.
source: SecurityWeek, 'Layoffs Hit Security Vendors Okta, Proofpoint, Netography' (Feb 2024)

name: Panther Labs
funding: over $220M raised; valued at roughly $1.1B in 2022
summary: Cloud-native, detection-as-code SIEM platform incorporating machine learning-driven analytics for large-scale security data. After reaching unicorn status in 2022, former employees describe a pattern of recurring layoffs euphemistically framed as 'restructures,' alongside frequent executive departures and strategic pivots, even as the company continued operating.
lesson: A unicorn valuation earned during a funding peak can mask an unsustainable burn rate; when growth slows, repeated 'restructures' rather than one transparent resizing erode both morale and investor confidence.
source: Glassdoor employee reviews, Panther Labs (2023-2024)

name: Darktrace
funding: ~$230M raised pre-IPO; IPO'd on LSE in 2021; acquired by Thoma Bravo for $5.3B in 2024
summary: Pioneer of self-learning AI for enterprise threat detection (the 'Enterprise Immune System'), using unsupervised machine learning to model normal network behavior and flag anomalies in real time. Darktrace grew into one of the most recognized AI-native cybersecurity brands, went public on the London Stock Exchange in 2021, and was acquired by private equity firm Thoma Bravo for $5.32 billion in 2024.
lesson: A distinctive, well-marketed AI narrative (the 'immune system' metaphor) combined with genuine anomaly-detection technology can carry a company from startup to public listing to a multi-billion-dollar buyout.
source: Bloomberg, 'Darktrace Agrees Sale to Thoma Bravo for $5.32 Billion' (Apr 2024)

name: Vectra AI
funding: $361M+ raised; $1.4B valuation as of a 2025 Series G led by Blackstone and TCV
summary: AI-driven network detection and response platform (Cognito/Attack Signal Intelligence) that applies machine learning to detect attacker behavior across hybrid cloud, identity and network environments. Vectra has scaled steadily as an independent private company, reaching a $1.4B valuation and $361M in cumulative funding as of late 2025 without an IPO or distressed exit.
lesson: Sustained focus on a single core detection category (network/identity threat detection) rather than broad platform sprawl let Vectra keep raising at increasing valuations through multiple funding cycles.
source: SDxCentral / company funding announcements, Vectra AI Series G (Oct 2025)

name: Abnormal Security
funding: $546M raised; $5.1B valuation as of August 2024 Series D
summary: AI-native email security platform using behavioral AI to model normal communication patterns across an organization and detect business email compromise, phishing, and account takeover without relying on static rules. Abnormal doubled its ARR to over $200M and closed a $250M round at a $5.1B valuation in 2024, positioning for an eventual IPO.
lesson: Building a genuinely AI-native product from day one -- rather than bolting ML onto legacy rule-based email filters -- let Abnormal outcompete incumbents and scale toward a public offering.
source: CNBC, 'Abnormal Security, valued at $5.1 billion amid email security push, eyes eventual IPO' (Aug 2024)

name: Deep Instinct
funding: $322M+ raised from investors including BlackRock and PayPal Ventures
summary: Deep-learning-first cybersecurity company that applies neural network models directly to raw file data to predict and prevent zero-day malware before execution, rather than relying on behavioral heuristics after the fact. Deep Instinct has continued to innovate through 2024, launching DIANNA, a generative-AI malware analysis assistant, while remaining an independent, well-funded private company.
lesson: Committing to a genuinely novel technical architecture (deep learning applied to raw binaries, rather than sandboxing or heuristics) can sustain differentiation and continued funding even a decade after founding.
source: SecurityWeek, Deep Instinct funding coverage; company product announcements (2024)

name: Balbix
funding: over $100M raised; acquired by Safe Security in November 2025
summary: AI-powered risk-based vulnerability management and cyber-risk quantification platform used to predict breach likelihood across an enterprise's attack surface. Balbix built products in partnership with Nvidia (BX4 engine) and launched a generative-AI assistant (BIX) before being acquired by Safe Security in late 2025 as part of continued market consolidation.
lesson: Positioning AI-driven risk quantification as a bridge between technical security teams and business/board-level risk reporting created a durable, differentiated niche that led to a strategic acquisition.
source: SiliconANGLE, 'Balbix's BX4 engine leverages Nvidia AI' (Mar 2024); Safe Security acquisition announcement (Nov 2025)

name: Forter
funding: $525M raised; valued at $3B
summary: AI-driven fraud prevention and identity trust platform used by e-commerce merchants to approve or decline transactions in real time using machine learning models trained on a shared merchant network. Forter has continued growing steadily, reporting more than $9B in incremental customer revenue driven and over $1B in customer cost savings in 2024.
lesson: A network-effect data model -- where every merchant's fraud signals improve detection for all merchants -- created a compounding moat that sustained growth over a decade without a forced exit.
source: Forter company blog, 2025 Frost & Sullivan Radar Report recognition

name: Feedzai
funding: $277.5M raised; valued at over $2B
summary: AI-native financial crime and fraud prevention platform (RiskOps) used by major banks and payment processors to detect fraud and money laundering with real-time machine learning scoring. Feedzai reached unicorn status and has continued expanding its behavioral-biometrics and transaction-monitoring capabilities for large financial institutions globally.
lesson: Focusing on the highest-stakes, highest-volume fraud use case (banking and payments) rather than diversifying early let Feedzai build defensible enterprise relationships that supported a multi-billion-dollar valuation.
source: Feedzai company site; industry valuation coverage

name: Riskified
funding: IPO'd on NYSE in 2021 at a $4.3B market cap
summary: AI-driven e-commerce fraud prevention platform offering a chargeback guarantee model, where Riskified assumes liability for approved transactions later found fraudulent. Riskified successfully completed an IPO in 2021 and has continued operating as a public company serving large online retailers, particularly luxury and high-value goods merchants.
lesson: Aligning incentives with customers through a financial guarantee (assuming chargeback liability) built enough merchant trust in the AI model's accuracy to support a successful public listing.
source: Riskified IPO coverage, NYSE listing (2021)

name: Signifyd
funding: $411M raised from FIS, Bain Capital Ventures, Menlo Ventures, American Express Ventures; valued at $1.34B
summary: AI-powered e-commerce fraud protection and payments optimization platform offering a guaranteed-fraud-protection model similar to Riskified. Signifyd reached unicorn status and remains an active, growing private company serving retail and e-commerce merchants with its Commerce Protection Platform.
lesson: Strategic investment from payment infrastructure players (FIS, Amex Ventures) alongside venture capital gave Signifyd distribution advantages that pure financial investors alone would not have provided.
source: Company funding profile; industry fraud-detection market coverage

name: PatternEx
funding: acquired by Corelight in October 2021
summary: AI-driven SIEM and automated threat-hunting platform combining more than 100 machine-learning detection models mapped to the MITRE ATT&CK framework, designed to reduce alert fatigue for security operations teams. PatternEx was acquired by network detection vendor Corelight in 2021 to add AI-driven detection capabilities to Corelight's network security offering.
lesson: Aligning an AI detection product explicitly to an industry-standard framework (MITRE ATT&CK) made PatternEx's technology easier for an acquirer to integrate and market, supporting a clean strategic exit.
source: Corelight/PatternEx acquisition announcement (Oct 2021)

name: Recorded Future
funding: acquired by Mastercard for $2.65B in 2024 (deal closed Dec 2024)
summary: AI-driven threat intelligence platform that analyzes massive volumes of open, dark web, and technical data to predict and prioritize cyber threats for over 1,900 clients including 45 national governments and half the Fortune 100. Insight Partners sold Recorded Future to Mastercard for $2.65B, one of the largest cybersecurity acquisitions of 2024, to bolster Mastercard's fraud and identity services.
lesson: Building the industry's most comprehensive threat-intelligence data asset over 15 years made Recorded Future strategically valuable well beyond traditional cybersecurity buyers, attracting a payments giant as acquirer.
source: Mastercard press release, 'Mastercard Invests in Continued Defense of Global Digital Economy With Acquisition of Recorded Future' (Sep 2024)

name: Interset
funding: acquired by Micro Focus for $89.7M in 2019
summary: Ottawa-based AI/machine-learning security analytics company providing predictive risk scoring for insider threats and account compromise. Micro Focus acquired Interset in 2019 to add its analytics engine to the ArcSight security portfolio; the technology later carried forward into OpenText's Core TDR product after OpenText acquired Micro Focus.
lesson: A specialized AI analytics engine can find a durable home inside successive larger security platforms (Micro Focus, then OpenText) long after the original startup's brand disappears.
source: SecurityWeek, 'Micro Focus Acquires Cyber-Threat Protection Firm Interset' (2019)

name: Fortscale
funding: $23M raised; acquired by RSA (Dell Technologies) in 2018
summary: User and entity behavior analytics (UEBA) startup using machine learning to detect compromised or abused accounts. RSA acquired Fortscale in 2018 to add native UEBA capabilities to its NetWitness evolved SIEM platform, a straightforward tuck-in acquisition by a major security vendor.
lesson: A focused UEBA technology with a clear integration story into an established SIEM platform made for an efficient, capital-light acquisition rather than requiring years of independent scaling.
source: PRNewswire, 'RSA Announces Intent to Acquire Fortscale' (2018)

name: Niddel
funding: acquired by Verizon in January 2018
summary: Machine-learning-based automated threat hunting platform founded by former NSA analysts. Verizon acquired Niddel in 2018 to integrate autonomous threat-hunting capabilities into its managed security services offerings, an acqui-hire-style exit for the small Miami-based team.
lesson: A specialized team with elite technical pedigree (ex-NSA) building a narrow, well-executed automation capability can achieve a clean acquisition exit even at modest scale.
source: CyberScoop, 'Verizon acquires startup Niddel and its automated threat hunting platform' (2018)

name: Confluera
funding: acquired by XM Cyber in March 2023
summary: Cloud extended detection and response (CxDR) startup using real-time sequencing and AI-driven correlation of attack steps across cloud environments. Confluera was acquired by attack-path-management vendor XM Cyber in 2023, combining Confluera's runtime detection with XM Cyber's exposure-management platform.
lesson: Pairing detection-focused AI technology with an exposure/attack-path-management acquirer created complementary product value that supported a successful, non-distressed exit.
source: Crunchbase acquisition record, XM Cyber acquires Confluera (Mar 2023)

name: Bay Dynamics
funding: acquired by Broadcom (via Symantec Enterprise Division) in December 2019
summary: Cyber risk analytics company whose Risk Fabric platform used user and entity behavior analytics to pinpoint emerging insider and account-compromise threats across existing security infrastructure. Broadcom acquired Bay Dynamics in 2019 as part of its broader Symantec Enterprise security acquisition strategy.
lesson: Behavioral risk-analytics technology that integrates with (rather than replaces) an enterprise's existing security stack is attractive to large platform acquirers assembling comprehensive suites.
source: ChannelE2E, 'Broadcom Buys Bay Dynamics' (2019)

name: Awake Security
funding: acquired by Arista Networks for $180.5M in October 2020
summary: AI-driven network detection and response platform that used machine learning to build a security 'graph' of entities and behaviors across enterprise networks. Arista Networks acquired Awake Security for $180.5M in cash in 2020 to build out its own network security services portfolio.
lesson: Network-hardware incumbents seeking to add AI-driven security analytics on top of their existing infrastructure created a natural, well-priced acquisition path for network detection startups.
source: Arista Networks 10-K (FY2020); acquisition press coverage

name: GreatHorn
funding: acquired by Proofpoint in August 2022
summary: Cloud-native email security platform using AI-driven detection to identify sophisticated phishing and business email compromise attacks across Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace. Proofpoint acquired GreatHorn in 2022 to strengthen its advanced threat protection capabilities, with GreatHorn's leadership integrating into Proofpoint's product organization.
lesson: Cloud-native email security vendors that build deep integrations with Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace remain attractive tuck-in targets for larger secure email gateway incumbents.
source: Proofpoint acquisition press release (Aug 2022)

name: ShieldX Networks
funding: acquired by Fortinet for $10.8M in March 2021
summary: Cloud security and micro-segmentation platform providing visibility and control across data centers and multi-cloud environments. Fortinet acquired ShieldX in 2021 to integrate its segmentation and cloud visibility technology into Fortinet's broader security fabric, a modest but clean strategic exit.
lesson: Even a relatively small-dollar acquisition ($10.8M) can be a successful outcome when it lets the underlying technology and team continue inside a much larger platform rather than shutting down.
source: Crunchbase acquisition record, Fortinet acquires ShieldX Networks (Mar 2021)

name: Bastille Networks
funding: privately funded; continues to operate and grow independently
summary: Wireless airspace threat-detection company using AI/ML analytics across the RF spectrum to detect rogue devices and wireless-based attacks against enterprise and government facilities. Bastille has remained an independent, profitable-trending company, named to the Inc. 5000 fastest-growing companies list for five consecutive years through 2025 with reported revenue growth of over 4,000%.
lesson: A narrow, hard-to-replicate technical niche (RF/wireless spectrum threat detection) with limited direct competition let Bastille grow steadily without needing repeated mega-rounds or an early exit.
source: Bastille Networks press release, Inc. 5000 recognition (2025)

name: Demisto
funding: acquired by Palo Alto Networks for $560M in March 2019
summary: Security orchestration, automation and response (SOAR) platform that used machine-learning-assisted playbooks to speed incident response. Palo Alto Networks acquired Demisto for $560M in 2019, one of the largest SOAR acquisitions at the time, forming the foundation of Palo Alto's Cortex XSOAR product line.
lesson: Building a SOAR platform with strong ML-assisted automation just as large platform vendors were racing to add orchestration capabilities positioned Demisto for a premium strategic acquisition.
source: TechCrunch, 'Palo Alto Networks to acquire Demisto for $560M' (2019)

name: Attivo Networks
funding: acquired by SentinelOne for $616.5M in May 2022
summary: Identity security and deception technology company that used decoys and behavioral analytics to detect lateral movement and identity-based attacks. SentinelOne acquired Attivo Networks for $616.5M in 2022 to add identity threat detection and response (ITDR) capabilities to its AI-driven XDR platform.
lesson: Deception technology combined with identity threat detection became a strategically valuable capability as ransomware attacks increasingly relied on lateral movement, making Attivo a premium acquisition target.
source: SEC filings, SentinelOne 10-Q (FY2022); Crunchbase acquisition record

name: Sqrrl
funding: acquired by Amazon Web Services for approximately $40M in January 2018
summary: Threat-hunting platform founded by former NSA engineers that combined link analysis and user behavior analytics into a visual threat-hunting tool. AWS acquired Sqrrl in 2018 to strengthen its cloud-based threat detection capabilities, an efficient exit for a company that had raised modest funding relative to the outcome.
lesson: A capital-efficient startup with deep technical expertise (former NSA engineers) doesn't need a huge valuation to deliver strong founder and investor returns relative to capital raised.
source: TechCrunch, 'AWS beefs up threat detection with Sqrrl acquisition' (2018)

name: Shape Security
funding: acquired by F5 Networks for approximately $1B in January 2020
summary: AI-driven bot mitigation and application fraud prevention company that used machine learning to distinguish automated credential-stuffing and scraping attacks from legitimate human traffic. F5 Networks acquired Shape Security for roughly $1 billion in 2020 to add advanced bot defense to its application security portfolio.
lesson: AI-based bot detection that can adapt faster than attackers can automate around it became a billion-dollar strategic capability as credential-stuffing and account-takeover fraud scaled industry-wide.
source: F5 Networks press release, 'F5 to Acquire Shape Security' (2020)

name: Kount
funding: acquired by Equifax for $640M in 2021
summary: AI-powered fraud prevention and identity trust platform serving e-commerce and payments companies with real-time transaction risk scoring. Equifax acquired Kount for $640M in 2021 to combine its identity data assets with Kount's machine-learning fraud detection, forming Equifax's fraud and identity business unit.
lesson: Pairing an AI fraud-scoring engine with a data-rich identity company (Equifax) created immediate cross-sell value that justified a substantial strategic premium.
source: Equifax acquisition press release (2021)

name: Argyle Data
funding: acquired by Mitel Mobile in 2018, later part of Mavenir
summary: Big-data machine-learning fraud-detection platform focused on telecom and mobile network fraud analytics. Argyle Data was acquired by Mitel Mobile in 2018 and its technology subsequently became part of Mavenir's telecom security offerings, a successful strategic exit rather than a shutdown.
lesson: A vertical-specific AI fraud-detection focus (telecom network fraud) made Argyle Data a natural fit for a telecom-infrastructure acquirer rather than requiring it to compete broadly against generalist fraud platforms.
source: PitchBook/Crunchbase company acquisition profile, Argyle Data

name: TrapX Security
funding: acquired by Commvault in 2023
summary: Deception technology platform (DeceptionGrid) using decoys and behavioral analytics to detect and analyze advanced persistent threats and ransomware lateral movement. Commvault acquired TrapX Security in 2023 to add early-warning ransomware detection capabilities to its data resilience and backup platform.
lesson: As ransomware defense became a board-level priority, deception technology found a natural home inside data-protection and resilience platforms looking to add pre-encryption detection capabilities.
source: MSSP Alert, Commvault acquires TrapX Security (2023)

name: Illusive Networks
funding: acquired by Proofpoint in 2022
summary: Deception and attack-surface-management technology that used AI-driven analytics to plant realistic decoys and detect lateral movement toward critical assets. Proofpoint acquired Illusive Networks in 2022 to extend its threat protection into identity-based attack surface reduction and post-compromise detection.
lesson: Extending an email/threat-protection platform into identity attack-surface reduction via acquisition let a deception-technology startup reach much broader enterprise distribution than it could alone.
source: Proofpoint acquisition announcement, Illusive Networks (2022)

name: Distil Networks
funding: acquired by Imperva in 2019
summary: AI/ML-driven bot detection and mitigation platform protecting websites, mobile apps and APIs from automated scraping, credential stuffing and fraud. Imperva acquired Distil Networks in 2019 to add advanced bot management to its application and data security portfolio.
lesson: Application security platform vendors consolidating bot-mitigation capabilities via acquisition validated bot detection as a must-have, durable category rather than a niche feature.
source: Imperva/Distil Networks acquisition coverage (2019)

name: HUMAN Security
funding: over $200M raised; merged with PerimeterX in 2022; remains a large private company
summary: AI-driven bot detection and fraud prevention platform (formerly White Ops) protecting advertising, e-commerce and enterprise applications from automated fraud using machine-learning-based device and behavioral fingerprinting. HUMAN Security merged with rival PerimeterX in 2022, becoming one of the largest independent bot-mitigation and digital fraud prevention companies.
lesson: Consolidating with a direct competitor (PerimeterX) rather than being acquired by a larger platform let HUMAN Security build scale and data advantages while remaining independently positioned for future growth or IPO.
source: HUMAN Security / PerimeterX merger announcement (2022)

name: Zimperium
funding: over $150M raised from investors including SoftBank and Samsung
summary: AI-powered mobile threat defense platform using on-device machine learning (z9 engine) to detect device, network, phishing and app-based attacks without requiring cloud connectivity. Zimperium has remained an independent, well-funded private company serving enterprise and government mobile security needs for over a decade.
lesson: Building on-device (rather than cloud-dependent) machine-learning detection created a durable technical differentiator in mobile security that sustained over a decade of independent growth.
source: Company funding history; industry mobile security market reporting

name: Skycure
funding: acquired by Symantec in 2017
summary: Mobile threat defense company using predictive, machine-learning-based analysis to detect network, app and OS-level threats on employee mobile devices. Symantec acquired Skycure in 2017 to add mobile threat defense to its broader enterprise endpoint security portfolio.
lesson: Enterprise endpoint security incumbents expanding into mobile-specific AI threat detection provided a reliable acquisition path for specialized mobile security startups.
source: Symantec/Skycure acquisition coverage (2017)

name: Claroty
funding: over $700M raised across multiple large growth rounds
summary: AI-driven industrial control systems (ICS) and operational technology (OT) security platform providing asset visibility, behavioral anomaly detection and threat detection for critical infrastructure environments. Claroty has grown into one of the largest independent OT/ICS security companies, raising substantial growth capital while remaining private.
lesson: Focusing exclusively on the underserved and high-stakes OT/ICS security niche -- rather than competing directly in crowded IT security markets -- let Claroty command premium enterprise and critical-infrastructure customer relationships.
source: Company funding history; industrial cybersecurity market coverage

name: Niara
funding: acquired by Hewlett Packard Enterprise in February 2017
summary: User and entity behavior analytics (UEBA) startup applying machine learning to detect insider threats and compromised accounts across enterprise networks. HPE acquired Niara in 2017 to strengthen its ClearPass network security portfolio with behavioral analytics capabilities.
lesson: Network infrastructure vendors seeking to differentiate their access-control products with AI-driven behavioral analytics created consistent acquisition demand for UEBA startups throughout the mid-2010s.
source: SiliconANGLE, 'HPE acquires Niara to boost its network security portfolio' (2017)

name: Seculert
funding: acquired by Radware in January 2017
summary: Cloud-based, machine-learning-driven threat detection platform that identified malware and advanced threats using big-data analytics on network traffic. Radware acquired Seculert in 2017 to add cloud-based threat detection to its application and network security portfolio.
lesson: A cloud-native, SaaS-delivered threat-detection architecture (rather than on-premises appliances) made Seculert an attractive, easily-integrated acquisition for a network security vendor expanding into cloud services.
source: SecurityWeek, 'Radware Acquires Threat Detection firm Seculert' (2017)
